4-15.e: In Y = a + bX: a = fixed cost (y-intercept), b = Variable Cost Rate (slope), X = activity level, Y = total cost. The slope 'b' shows how much cost changes per unit of activity.

4-15.q: The scatter diagram method is Subjective because:
o: Different analysts may draw different best-fit lines
4-15.e: Scatter diagram is Subjective — different people looking at the same data plot may draw different best-fit lines through the data, yielding different results. Regression analysis gives an objective mathematical answer.

4-15.q: An R-squared (R2) of 0.15 indicates:
o: Only 15% of cost variation explained — weak relationship
4-15.e: R2 = 0.15 means only 15% of cost variation is explained by the cost driver — a Weak relationship. The remaining 85% is explained by other factors. This driver is poor — find a better one with higher R2.

4-15.q: An 80% learning curve: 1st unit = 100 hours. Output doubles to 2 units. Average time per unit =
4-15.e: 80% learning: when cumulative output Doubles, average time per unit = previous average x 80% = 100 x 80% = 80 hours per unit. The 2 units will average 80 hours each (total 160 hours for both units).

4-15.q: The Accounts Analysis method estimates costs by:
o: Classifying each account as fixed or variable based on judgment
4-15.e: Accounts Analysis: a cost analyst reviews Each Account in the general ledger and uses professional judgment to classify each cost as fixed or variable. It is simple but Subjective — relies on the analyst's expertise.

4-15.q: High-Low: High 10,000 units/$50,000. Low 4,000 units/$26,000. Variable rate =
4-15.e: Variable Rate = (High Cost - Low Cost) / (High Activity - Low Activity) = ($50,000 - $26,000) / (10,000 - 4,000) = $24,000 / 6,000 = $4.00 per unit.

4-15.q: High-Low Method: High point = 10,000 units / $50,000. Low point = 4,000 units / $26,000. Variable rate = $4.00/unit. Fixed Cost =
4-15.e: Fixed Cost = Total Cost at High point − (Variable Rate × High Activity) = $50,000 − ($4 × 10,000) = $50,000 − $40,000 = $10,000. Verify using Low point: $26,000 − ($4 × 4,000) = $10,000. ✓

4-15.q: Multiple regression is preferred over simple regression when:
o: Costs are driven by multiple factors simultaneously
4-15.e: Multiple regression is better when costs are driven by Two Or More factors simultaneously. Example: Total cost = $5,000 + $3(machine hours) + $2(labor hours). Using both drivers gives more accurate predictions than either alone.

4-15.q: The learning curve effect is Most significant for:
o: Simple, repetitive tasks requiring no skill
Complex, labor-intensive tasks where workers improve with practice
4-15.e: Learning curve is most significant for Complex, Labor-Intensive tasks where workers improve with practice. Highly automated processes show little learning effect because machines do not 'learn' — humans do.

4-15.q: Under the Incremental Unit-Time model (80% learning curve), if the 1st unit takes 100 hours, the 2nd unit takes:
4-15.e: Incremental Unit-Time Model: when cumulative output Doubles, the Last (incremental) unit takes 80% of the previous last unit's time. 1st unit = 100 hrs. 2nd unit = 100 × 80% = 80 hrs. The 4th unit = 80 × 80% = 64 hrs. Compare to Cumulative Average Model where 80% applies to the Average of all units.

4-15.q: The Key difference between Cumulative Average-Time and Incremental Unit-Time learning curve models is:
o: Whether 80% applies to the Average of all units (cumulative) or to the Last unit produced (incremental)
The industry type where each applies
How many times output must double
4-15.e: Cumulative Average: the 80% rate applies to the Average time per unit when cumulative output doubles. Incremental Unit: the 80% rate applies to the Time For The Most Recent Unit when output doubles. Both use the same rate but calculate differently — the cumulative average model generally results in higher total time estimates.

4-16.q: The Bullwhip Effect in supply chain management refers to:
o: Small demand changes causing amplified inventory fluctuations upstream
The effect of price discounts on demand
Supply chain disruptions caused by weather
4-16.e: The Bullwhip Effect: small variations in end-customer demand become Amplified as you move upstream in the supply chain. Retailers order more → wholesalers order even more → manufacturers overproduce → excess inventory throughout the chain.

4-16.q: In Porter's Value Chain, Operations is classified as a:
4-16.e: Operations is a Primary activity in Porter's Value Chain. Primary activities directly create value: Inbound Logistics → Operations → Outbound Logistics → Marketing & Sales → Service. Support activities (HR, IT, Procurement) enable primary activities.

4-16.q: A customer waiting in line at a bank is an example of:
4-16.e: Customer waiting is a Non-Value-Added activity — the customer gets no value from waiting and would not pay for it if given the choice. It is pure waste that should be eliminated or minimized.

4-16.q: Benchmarking is best defined as:
o: Comparing performance against best-in-class standards or competitors
4-16.e: Benchmarking = comparing your performance, processes, or products against Best-In-Class standards (internal benchmarks, competitor benchmarks, or industry best practices) to identify improvement opportunities.

4-16.q: Business Process Reengineering (Bpr) differs from Kaizen because Bpr involves:
o: Radical redesign for dramatic performance improvement
4-16.e: Bpr = Radical, fundamental redesign of processes to achieve dramatic improvements (50%+ gains). Kaizen = small, continuous, incremental improvements. Bpr asks 'Should we do this at all?' Kaizen asks 'How can we do this better?'

4-16.q: Procurement (purchasing) in Porter's Value Chain is classified as a:
4-16.e: Procurement is a Support activity in Porter's Value Chain. Support activities enable primary activities: Firm Infrastructure, HR Management, Technology Development, and Procurement. They don't directly create customer value but are necessary.

4-16.q: The Goal of Supply Chain Management is to:
o: Minimize the company's own costs only
Maximize the profits of each individual supply chain member
Optimize the performance of the Entire supply chain
Eliminate all suppliers and vertically integrate
4-16.e: Scm goal = optimize the Entire supply chain from raw materials to end customer. This may require individual companies to accept slightly higher costs if it benefits the overall chain performance (speed, reliability, total cost).

4-16.q: Vendor-Managed Inventory (Vmi) means:
o: The company manages all its own inventory internally
The supplier monitors and replenishes the customer's inventory
Inventory is managed by a third-party logistics provider
Customers track their own orders online
4-16.e: Vmi = the Supplier takes responsibility for monitoring and replenishing the customer's inventory levels. The supplier has access to inventory data and ensures stock is always available. This reduces customer ordering burden and can reduce bullwhip effect.

4-16.q: The Dmaic methodology is associated with:
4-16.e: Dmaic (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) is the structured problem-solving methodology used in Six Sigma projects. It provides a systematic approach to improving processes by identifying root causes and implementing data-driven solutions.

4-16.q: An activity that the customer would willingly pay for because it changes the product's form, fit, or function is called:
4-16.e: Value-Added activities are those customers recognize as important and would willingly pay for — they change the product's form, fit, or function. Non-value-added activities consume resources but add no customer value.

4-16.q: Just-in-Time (JIT) is best described as a:
o: Push production system based on forecasts
Pull production system based on actual demand
4-16.e: JIT is a Pull system — production is triggered by actual customer demand, not forecasts. Materials and products are made only when needed, in the exact quantity needed. Traditional systems are Push (produce to forecast).

4-16.q: The Primary goal of JIT inventory management is to:
4-16.e: JIT aims to reduce inventory to Near-Zero levels — having inventory only at the exact moment needed. Excess inventory is considered Waste (ties up capital, hides problems, takes storage space).

4-16.q: In the 7 wastes (Timwood), 'I' stands for:
4-16.e: In Timwood, I = Inventory. Excess inventory is waste because it ties up capital, requires storage space, can become obsolete, and hides production problems. JIT aims to eliminate excess inventory.

4-16.q: Kanban is best described as:
o: A signal/card system authorizing production or material movement
A method for calculating overhead rates
4-16.e: Kanban is the Signal System used in JIT — cards (kanban) that authorize the production or movement of materials only when needed. It prevents overproduction by limiting work-in-process.

4-16.q: The Theory of Constraints (Toc) focuses on:
o: Eliminating all waste in the system
Identifying and managing the bottleneck constraint
4-16.e: Toc focuses on identifying and managing the Bottleneck — the single constraint limiting overall system throughput. Improving non-bottleneck processes provides no benefit unless the bottleneck is addressed first.

4-16.q: Compared to traditional manufacturing, JIT typically requires:
4-16.e: JIT requires Fewer, Highly Reliable Suppliers with long-term partnerships. Since JIT operates with minimal inventory, any supply disruption halts production. Supplier reliability and quality are critical.

4-16.q: The Main Risk of JIT is:
o: Production halted by supply chain disruptions
4-16.e: JIT's main risk is Supply Chain Disruption. With near-zero inventory, any supplier delay or quality problem can immediately halt production. This was dramatically demonstrated during Covid-19 supply chain disruptions.

4-16.q: Lean management originated from:
4-16.e: Lean management originated from the Toyota Production System (Tps), developed by Taiichi Ohno and Shigeo Shingo at Toyota. It became known as 'lean' when documented by Mit researchers studying Toyota's superior manufacturing.

4-16.q: Overproduction is considered a waste in lean management because:
o: It produces more than customer demand requires, tying up resources
4-16.e: Overproduction is waste because it produces More than customers need Now — creating excess inventory, tying up capital, consuming materials and labor unnecessarily, and potentially leading to obsolescence.

4-16.q: In Toc, 'throughput' is defined as:
o: Sales revenue minus all variable costs
Sales revenue minus direct material costs
4-16.e: Toc Throughput = Sales Revenue - Direct Material costs (the truly variable costs). Toc considers only direct materials as truly variable in the short run. Labor and overhead are treated as fixed. Maximizing throughput at the bottleneck maximizes profits.

4-17.q: An ERP system differs from traditional separate systems primarily because ERP:
o: Integrates all business functions into one unified database
Is used only for manufacturing companies
Replaces the need for management accountants
4-17.e: ERP integrates All business functions (finance, manufacturing, HR, sales, procurement) into One unified database, eliminating data silos and providing real-time, consistent information across the entire organization.

4-17.q: The Master Production Schedule (Mps) in MRP specifies:
o: The list of all materials needed per product
What finished products to produce and when
4-17.e: The Mps specifies What finished products to produce, in what Quantities, and When. It is the primary input to MRP, which uses the Mps to calculate material requirements.

4-17.q: The Bill of Materials (Bom) contains:
o: The complete list of components and quantities needed to make one unit of a product
4-17.e: Bom = complete list of All components, subassemblies, and raw materials (with quantities) needed to produce One unit of the finished product. It is a key input to MRP calculations.

4-17.q: Gross Requirements 500 units. On-hand inventory 120. Scheduled receipts 80. Net Requirements =
4-17.e: Net Requirements = Gross - On Hand - Scheduled Receipts = 500 - 120 - 80 = 300 units need to be ordered or produced.

4-17.q: MRP Ii extends MRP by adding:
o: Capacity planning, labor scheduling, and financial integration
4-17.e: MRP Ii extends MRP beyond materials to include Capacity Planning (are machines/labor available?), labor scheduling, machine scheduling, and financial planning — integrating manufacturing with the broader business.

4-17.q: MRP is classified as a _____ production system:
4-17.e: MRP is a Push system — production is based on Forecasts and schedules. Products are pushed through production based on planned demand. JIT is a Pull system — production is triggered by actual customer demand.

4-17.q: The Biggest risk of implementing an ERP system is:
o: The system will automate too many jobs
Implementation failure causing cost overruns and business disruption
The system will not connect to the internet
Accounting standards will not be followed
4-17.e: ERP implementation failure is a major risk — projects frequently exceed budget (sometimes by 100%+), take longer than planned, and can disrupt business operations. Many high-profile ERP failures have caused significant financial damage to companies.

4-17.q: Which of the following is an example of an ERP system?
4-17.e: Sap S/4HANA is a major ERP system. Other examples: Oracle ERP Cloud, Microsoft Dynamics 365. QuickBooks is a small business accounting system (not a full ERP). Excel is a spreadsheet tool.

4-17.q: In an MRP system, 'Scheduled Receipts' refers to:
o: Orders already placed and expected to arrive
4-17.e: Scheduled Receipts = orders already placed with suppliers (or already in production) that are expected to Arrive during the planning period. They reduce net requirements because they represent incoming supply.

4-17.q: What is the correct order of scope from narrowest to broadest?
4-17.e: MRP (materials only) → MRP Ii (materials + manufacturing resources) → ERP (entire enterprise — all business functions). Each builds on and extends the previous, with ERP being the most comprehensive.

4-17.q: MRP's primary purpose is to determine:
o: What materials to order, how much, and when
How to integrate all business functions
How to manage machine maintenance schedules
4-17.e: MRP determines What materials to order, How Much to order, and When to order them — based on the Master Production Schedule (Mps), Bill of Materials (Bom), and current inventory records. It is a forecast-driven Push system.

4-17.q: The Bill of Materials (Bom) contains:
o: All components and quantities needed to make one unit of finished product
4-17.e: Bom = complete list of All raw materials, components, and subassemblies (with quantities) needed to produce One finished unit. It is a Key input to MRP for calculating material requirements.

4-17.q: MRP is classified as a ___ production system:
4-17.e: MRP is a Push system — production is based on Forecasts and schedules from the Master Production Schedule. Products are pushed through production based on planned demand, unlike JIT (pull system triggered by actual demand).

4-17.q: Net Requirements = Gross Requirements - On-hand Inventory - ___
4-17.e: Net Requirements = Gross Requirements - On-hand Inventory - Scheduled Receipts. Scheduled receipts are orders already placed and expected to arrive. This calculates how many additional units must be ordered or produced.

4-17.q: MRP Ii extends MRP by adding:
o: Capacity planning, labor scheduling, and financial integration
4-17.e: MRP Ii extends MRP beyond materials to include Capacity Planning (can we actually produce this?), labor scheduling, machine scheduling, and financial planning — integrating the manufacturing plan with broader business resources.

4-17.q: ERP differs from MRP because ERP integrates:
o: All business functions into one unified database
4-17.e: ERP integrates All business functions — finance, manufacturing, HR, sales, procurement, inventory — into One unified database. This eliminates data silos and provides real-time, consistent information across the entire organization.

4-17.q: Gross Requirements 800. On-hand 200. Scheduled Receipts 100. Net Requirements =
4-17.e: Net Requirements = Gross Requirements - On-hand - Scheduled Receipts = 800 - 200 - 100 = 500 units. This is the quantity that must be ordered or produced to meet the planned demand.

4-17.q: The Master Production Schedule (Mps) specifies:
o: What finished products to produce, in what quantity, and when
4-17.e: Mps = the production plan specifying What finished products to make, in what Quantities, and When. It drives the entire MRP calculation — MRP uses the Mps to determine what materials are needed and when.

4-17.q: The Biggest Risk of ERP implementation is:
o: Implementation failure causing major cost overruns and business disruption
The system connects too many departments
4-17.e: ERP implementation Failure is a major risk — projects frequently exceed budget by 100%+, take longer than planned, and can Disrupt business operations. Many companies have suffered major financial damage from failed ERP implementations.

4-17.q: Correct scope progression from Narrowest to Broadest:
o: ERP then MRP Ii then MRP
MRP Ii then ERP then MRP
MRP then MRP Ii then ERP
ERP then MRP then MRP Ii
4-17.e: MRP (materials only) then MRP Ii (materials + manufacturing resources) then ERP (entire enterprise — all business functions). Each builds on and extends the previous system, with ERP being most comprehensive.

4-18.q: Which capacity concept represents the maximum output under perfect conditions with no downtime?
4-18.e: Theoretical Capacity = maximum output 24/7 under Perfect conditions — no breakdowns, no scheduled maintenance, no employee breaks. It is a theoretical benchmark never actually achieved in practice.

4-18.q: A sunk cost is:
o: A future cost relevant to the decision
A past cost that cannot be changed — always irrelevant
A variable cost that changes with production
4-18.e: Sunk costs are Past costs already incurred that cannot be recovered regardless of the decision made. They are Always Irrelevant in decision-making because they do not differ between alternatives.

4-18.q: Outside purchase price $18/unit. Variable cost to make $14/unit. Avoidable fixed costs $3/unit. Should the company make or buy?
4-18.e: Avoidable cost to make = $14 variable + $3 avoidable fixed = $17/unit. Outside price = $18. Make costs Less ($17 < $18) → Make and save $1/unit.

4-18.q: A special order should be Accepted when:
o: The special price exceeds the full absorption cost
The special price exceeds variable cost And excess capacity exists
The special price equals the regular selling price
Fixed costs are covered by the special order
4-18.e: Accept special order if: Special price > Variable cost per unit And there is Excess Capacity. Fixed costs are irrelevant (already covered by regular sales). The order contributes positive Cm.

4-18.q: When deciding to Drop a product line, which costs are Irrelevant?
4-18.e: Allocated (common) fixed costs are Irrelevant in keep-or-drop decisions because they continue regardless of whether the segment is dropped — they will just be reallocated to remaining segments. Only Avoidable costs matter.

4-18.q: When a company has a scarce resource (constraint), products should be ranked by:
o: Highest Cm per unit of the scarce resource
4-18.e: With a constraint, rank by Cm Per Unit Of The Scarce Resource. A product with lower Cm per unit may be more profitable if it uses less of the constrained resource. Example: Cm $10 using 5 machine hours = $2/Mh vs Cm $8 using 2 Mh = $4/Mh → prefer the second.

4-18.q: Opportunity cost is:
o: A sunk cost from a previous decision
The cost of the next best alternative foregone
Equal to the variable cost of production
4-18.e: Opportunity cost = the benefit Given Up by choosing one alternative over the next best option. It is Always relevant because it represents the true economic cost of a decision. Example: using a machine for Product A means foregoing the contribution margin from Product B.

4-18.q: Normal capacity is used to calculate Pohr because:
o: It is the maximum possible capacity
It smooths out seasonal fluctuations over a longer period
4-18.e: Normal capacity (2-3 year average) is used for Pohr because it smooths out seasonal and cyclical fluctuations, providing a stable overhead rate. Using actual capacity would cause overhead rates to fluctuate wildly between periods.

4-18.q: Variable cost $20/unit. Fixed costs $50,000 (total). Special order 1,000 units at $24/unit. Excess capacity exists. Accept or reject?
o: Accept — only if fixed costs are waived
4-18.e: Special order analysis: Price ($24) > Variable cost ($20) → Cm = $4/unit. Total contribution = $4 x 1,000 = $4,000. Fixed costs are Irrelevant (already covered). Accept the order — it adds $4,000 to profit.

4-18.q: Product A: Cm $30, requires 3 machine hours. Product B: Cm $40, requires 5 machine hours. Machine hours are the constraint. Which product should be prioritized?
o: Product B — higher Cm per unit
Product A — higher Cm per machine hour
Product B — requires more machine hours
4-18.e: Product A: Cm per Mh = $30/3 = $10/Mh. Product B: Cm per Mh = $40/5 = $8/Mh. Product A generates More contribution margin per machine hour → Prioritize Product A when machine hours are constrained.

4-18.q: Practical Capacity differs from Theoretical Capacity because Practical Capacity:
o: Subtracts normal downtime, maintenance, and breaks
Is used for tax reporting only
4-18.e: Practical Capacity = Theoretical Capacity - Normal downtime (scheduled maintenance, employee breaks, holidays). It is a Realistic maximum achievable output. Theoretical capacity is a benchmark never truly achievable in practice.

4-18.q: Normal Capacity is used for Pohr because it:
o: Smooths seasonal fluctuations over 2-3 years for a stable rate
4-18.e: Normal Capacity (2-3 year average) is used for Pohr because it Smooths seasonal and cyclical fluctuations, providing a Stable overhead rate throughout the year. Using actual capacity would cause overhead rates to fluctuate wildly.

4-18.q: In a Make-or-Buy decision, allocated fixed costs are:
4-18.e: Allocated (common) fixed costs are Irrelevant in Make-or-Buy — they continue even if the company outsources (just reallocated elsewhere). Only Avoidable costs matter — costs that actually disappear if production stops.

4-18.q: A special order should be Rejected when:
o: Price exceeds variable cost with excess capacity
4-18.e: Reject a special order if: Special Price < Variable Cost per unit. If the price does not even cover variable costs, each unit sold Loses money. Accept only if Price > Variable Cost And excess capacity is available.

4-18.q: In Keep-or-Drop decisions, which cost is Always irrelevant?
4-18.e: Allocated (common) fixed costs are Always irrelevant in Keep-or-Drop — they do not disappear when the segment is dropped. They are simply reallocated to remaining segments. Only avoidable costs and lost Cm matter.

4-18.q: Product A: Cm $20, uses 4 constraint hours. Product B: Cm $15, uses 2 constraint hours. Priority goes to:
o: Product A — higher Cm per unit
Product B — higher Cm per constraint hour
4-18.e: A: $20/4 = $5/hr. B: $15/2 = $7.50/hr. Prioritize Product B — it generates More contribution margin per scarce constraint hour. Rank by Cm Per Unit Of Constraint, not Cm per unit.

4-18.q: $100,000 already spent on R&D for a product. This cost should ___ influence the decision to continue:
4-18.e: The $100,000 is a Sunk Cost — Irrelevant. It cannot be recovered regardless of the decision. Only future incremental costs and revenues should drive the decision. Never let sunk costs influence future decisions.

4-18.q: Outside price $22/unit. Variable cost $18. Avoidable fixed $2. Allocated fixed $4. Make or Buy decision:
4-18.e: Avoidable cost to make = $18 variable + $2 avoidable fixed = $20/unit. Allocated $4 is Irrelevant. Outside price = $22. Make ($20) < Buy ($22) → Make and save $2/unit.

4-18.q: Special order 500 units at $15. Variable cost $12. 500 units excess capacity available. Incremental profit =
4-18.e: Cm per unit = $15 - $12 = $3. Total contribution = $3 x 500 = $1,500. Fixed costs are Irrelevant (excess capacity already covers them). The special order adds $1,500 to profit — Accept.

4-18.q: With Excess Capacity, fixed costs are ___ in a special order decision:
o: Irrelevant — already covered by regular sales
Must always be allocated to special order
4-18.e: With Excess Capacity, fixed costs are Already Covered by regular sales. The special order uses otherwise idle capacity — no additional fixed costs are incurred. Only variable costs need to be covered for the order to be profitable.

4-19.q: When evaluating an outsourcing decision, which costs are Relevant?
o: All fixed costs including allocated overhead
Avoidable variable and fixed costs only
4-19.e: Only Avoidable costs are relevant in outsourcing decisions — costs that will be eliminated if production stops (variable costs + avoidable fixed costs). Allocated/unavoidable fixed costs continue regardless and are Irrelevant.

4-19.q: Variable cost to make $25/unit. Avoidable fixed cost $3/unit. Allocated fixed cost $5/unit. Outside price $30. Should the company outsource?
4-19.e: Avoidable cost to make = $25 + $3 = $28/unit (allocated $5 is irrelevant). Outside price = $30. Keep making ($28 < $30) — outsourcing costs $2 More per unit.

4-19.q: A company should generally Not outsource activities that are:
4-19.e: Core Competencies — activities that provide strategic competitive advantage — should Not be outsourced. Outsourcing them risks losing the capability, sharing proprietary knowledge with competitors, and losing strategic differentiation.

4-19.q: A cost leadership strategy focuses on:
o: Offering unique products customers pay premium for
Being the lowest-cost producer in the industry
Maximizing product quality regardless of cost
4-19.e: Cost Leadership strategy = being the Lowest-Cost Producer. Companies like Walmart pursue cost leadership by aggressively reducing costs through efficiency, economies of scale, and tight cost control throughout the value chain.

4-19.q: A differentiation strategy implies that cost management should:
o: Invest in costs that support the unique value offered to customers
4-19.e: Differentiation strategy: Invest in costs that support the unique value customers pay a premium for — quality, R&D, customer service, brand. Cutting these costs would undermine the differentiation and destroy competitive advantage.

4-19.q: Customer profitability analysis assigns costs to customers based on:
4-19.e: Customer profitability uses ABC principles to assign costs based on Resources Consumed by each customer — ordering frequency, returns, customer service calls, special requirements, etc. Some customers are highly profitable; others are unprofitable despite high revenue.

4-19.q: The Balanced Scorecard's Financial perspective answers the question:
o: What must we excel at internally?
How do we look to shareholders?
4-19.e: Financial perspective asks: 'How do we look to Shareholders?' — focusing on financial results like revenue growth, profitability, and ROI. The other perspectives are Customer ('How do customers see us?'), Internal Process ('What must we excel at?'), and Learning & Growth ('Can we continue to improve?').

4-19.q: Supply chain disruption risk is a major Qualitative factor against:
4-19.e: Supply chain disruption is a key Risk Of Outsourcing. When an external supplier fails to deliver (due to financial problems, natural disasters, capacity issues), the buying company has no backup production capability. This is especially critical for JIT manufacturers.

4-19.q: Accounting process redesign under lean primarily aims to:
o: Increase the number of journal entries for greater accuracy
Eliminate non-value-added accounting tasks and align reporting with lean operations
Require more detailed monthly variance analysis
Expand standard costing to all departments
4-19.e: Lean accounting process redesign eliminates non-value-added accounting work (unused reports, excessive transactions), simplifies recording (backflush costing), and aligns the accounting system with lean operations — so accounting Supports lean behaviour rather than contradicting it.

4-19.q: Traditional standard costing can conflict with lean manufacturing because it:
o: Rewards production volume — managers produce more to absorb fixed overhead, building up inventory
4-19.e: Traditional absorption costing allocates fixed overhead per unit — so Producing More units lowers unit cost and improves reported performance. This incentivises Overproduction and inventory build-up, directly contradicting lean's goal of zero inventory and pull-based production.

4-19.q: Backflush costing supports lean/JIT because it:
o: Records costs at every stage of production separately
Records costs only at completion or sale — eliminating complex Wip tracking
Increases the number of accounting transactions
4-19.e: Backflush costing Delays cost recording until products are completed or sold, then 'flushes' costs back. This eliminates the complex, time-consuming tracking of costs through multiple Wip stages — supporting JIT's goal of simplified, streamlined operations.

4-19.q: A customer generates $100,000 revenue with 40% gross margin but requires $45,000 in special service costs. This customer is:
4-19.e: Gross margin = 40% x $100,000 = $40,000. Cost to serve = $45,000. Customer profitability = $40,000 - $45,000 = -$5,000. This customer is Unprofitable — the company should consider repricing, reducing service levels, or dropping this customer.

4-19.q: The Learning & Growth perspective of the Balanced Scorecard focuses on:
o: Employee skills, training, and innovation capacity
4-19.e: Learning & Growth perspective: 'Can we continue to improve and create value?' — focuses on Employee Capabilities, training hours, innovation rate, technology infrastructure, and organizational learning. It is the Foundation that enables the other three perspectives.

4-19.q: Outsourcing decision rule: Outsource if Outside Cost < ___
4-19.e: Outsource if: Outside Cost < Total Avoidable Internal Cost (variable costs + avoidable fixed costs). Allocated/unavoidable fixed costs are Irrelevant — they continue regardless of the outsourcing decision.

4-19.q: A company should generally Not outsource its:
4-19.e: Core Competencies — activities providing strategic competitive advantage — should Not be outsourced. Risks include: losing the capability, sharing proprietary knowledge with competitors, and destroying the strategic differentiation.

4-19.q: A cost leadership strategy requires cost management to focus on:
o: Investing heavily in quality and innovation
Being the Lowest-Cost Producer through efficiency
Maximizing product uniqueness regardless of cost
4-19.e: Cost Leadership = be the Lowest-Cost Producer. Cost management must aggressively reduce costs through process efficiency, economies of scale, overhead reduction, and supply chain optimization throughout the value chain.

4-19.q: Under a differentiation strategy, cutting costs that Support the unique value offered would:
o: Undermine the differentiation and destroy competitive advantage
4-19.e: Differentiation strategy = offer unique value customers pay a premium for. Cutting the costs that Create this differentiation (quality, R&D, service) would Destroy the competitive advantage. Not all cost cutting is positive under differentiation.

4-19.q: Customer Profitability = Contribution Margin from customer - ___
4-19.e: Customer Profitability = Contribution Margin earned From the customer - Cost To Serve that customer (ordering costs, returns, customer service calls, special requirements, etc.). Some high-revenue customers are unprofitable when full service costs are considered.

4-19.q: The Internal Process perspective of the BSC answers:
o: How do we look to shareholders?
What must we Excel At Internally to satisfy customers?
4-19.e: Internal Process perspective: 'What must we Excel At Internally?' — focuses on key internal processes like quality, cycle time, production efficiency, and innovation that create customer and shareholder value.

4-19.q: Lean accounting process redesign aims to Eliminate:
o: Non-value-added accounting tasks and reports nobody uses
4-19.e: Lean accounting redesign eliminates Non-Value-Added accounting work — reports nobody reads, excessive transactions, redundant reconciliations. The goal: make accounting Lean too — faster, simpler, more relevant to operations.

4-19.q: A Strategy Map in the Balanced Scorecard shows:
o: The organisation's financial results for the year
Cause-Effect Relationships between the four BSC perspectives — how non-financial drivers lead to financial outcomes
A map of the company's facilities
4-19.e: Strategy Maps show the Cause-Effect chain linking BSC perspectives: Learning & Growth drives better Internal Processes → which improves Customer outcomes → which drives Financial results. They make strategy Visible and testable.

4-19.q: Leading indicators in the BSC are:
o: Predictors of future performance — drivers that will cause future financial results
4-19.e: Leading indicators Predict future performance (e.g., employee training hours, R&D investment, customer satisfaction). Lagging indicators Reflect past performance (e.g., revenue, net income). BSC uses Both to provide a complete picture.

4-19.q: The Learning & Growth perspective of the BSC is important because:
o: IT IS The Foundation — employee skills and organisational capabilities enable all other perspectives
4-19.e: Learning & Growth = the foundation of the BSC strategy map. Employee capabilities, training, technology, and organisational culture Enable better internal processes, which serve customers better, which drives financial results. Without L&G investment, the other perspectives deteriorate over time.

4-20.q: Takt Time is calculated as:
o: Total production time / Number of workers
Available production time / Customer demand rate
Cycle time x Number of units
4-20.e: Takt Time = Available Production Time / Customer Demand Rate. It sets the Production Pace needed to meet customer demand. If takt time = 4 minutes, one unit must be completed every 4 minutes.

4-20.q: Available time 600 minutes/day. Customer demand 150 units/day. Takt Time =
4-20.e: Takt Time = 600 / 150 = 4 minutes per unit. The production process must complete one unit every 4 minutes to exactly meet customer demand without overproducing.

4-20.q: Lead Time differs from Cycle Time because Lead Time:
o: Includes total time from order to delivery, including waiting
Is always shorter than cycle time
4-20.e: Lead Time = Total time from customer order to delivery — including all waiting, inspection, transport, and processing time. Cycle Time = time to complete one unit in production. Lead Time is almost always longer than Cycle Time due to non-value-added waiting.

4-20.q: Value Stream Mapping (Vsm) shows:
o: Both material and information flows from supplier to customer
4-20.e: Vsm maps the Entire flow of Both materials (physical product flow) And information (orders, schedules, signals) from supplier through the company to the customer. It reveals waste in the entire value stream.

4-20.q: The Value-Added Ratio is calculated as:
4-20.e: Value-Added Ratio = Process Time / Lead Time. It shows what percentage of total lead time is actually spent adding value. A ratio of 10% means only 10% of time is value-added — 90% is waste (waiting, transport, inspection, etc.).

4-20.q: In process analysis, a 'Current State Map' documents:
o: The process as it exists Today — including all waste
4-20.e: Current State Map documents the process As IT IS Today — including all waste, inefficiencies, waiting time, excess inventory, and non-value-added activities. It is the starting point for identifying improvement opportunities.

4-20.q: The Bottleneck in a process is:
o: The slowest step that limits overall throughput
The step with the most workers
4-20.e: The bottleneck is the Slowest step — the constraint that limits the rate of the entire system. Overall throughput cannot exceed the bottleneck's capacity. All upstream processes accumulate inventory waiting for the bottleneck.

4-20.q: Improving a non-bottleneck process step will:
o: Have no effect on overall throughput
4-20.e: Improving a Non-Bottleneck step does Not increase overall throughput — the bottleneck still limits output. It may create more inventory piling up in front of the bottleneck. Only improving the Bottleneck increases system throughput.

4-20.q: The correct order of the 5 Toc steps is:
4-20.e: Toc 5 Steps: (1) Identify the constraint, (2) Exploit — get maximum output from it, (3) Subordinate — align all other processes to support it, (4) Elevate — invest to increase its capacity, (5) Repeat — find the next constraint.

4-20.q: Under throughput costing, which costs are treated as Product costs?
o: Direct Materials and Direct Labor only
Direct Materials, Direct Labor, and Variable Overhead
All manufacturing costs including fixed overhead
4-20.e: Throughput costing (super-variable costing) treats Only Direct Materials as product costs. Direct Labor and All overhead costs (both fixed and variable) are treated as period costs. This reflects Toc's view that only DM is truly variable in the short run.

4-20.q: Throughput Contribution is calculated as:
o: Sales Revenue - Direct Materials Cost only
Sales Revenue - Cost of Goods Sold
4-20.e: Throughput Contribution = Sales Revenue - Direct Materials Cost Only. Unlike contribution margin (which subtracts All variable costs), throughput contribution only subtracts direct materials — treating DL and OH as fixed period costs.

4-20.q: Product A: Sales $120, DM $60, bottleneck hours 2. Product B: Sales $90, DM $30, bottleneck hours 1. Which should be prioritised?
o: Product A — higher total throughput contribution
Product B — higher throughput per bottleneck hour
4-20.e: Product A throughput/hour = ($120-$60)/2 = $30/hr. Product B throughput/hour = ($90-$30)/1 = $60/hr. Prioritise Product B because it generates more throughput per scarce bottleneck hour — even though Product A has a higher total throughput contribution ($60 vs $60).

4-20.q: After successfully elevating a bottleneck, a Toc manager should:
o: Stop improvement efforts — the constraint is resolved
Return to Step 1 and identify the New constraint
Focus on reducing costs in all departments equally
Implement standard costing across the system
4-20.e: Toc Step 5 is Repeat — once you elevate the current bottleneck, a New constraint will emerge elsewhere in the system. The manager must return to Step 1 and identify the new bottleneck. Continuous constraint management is the Toc philosophy.

4-20.q: Process Time 30 minutes. Lead Time 300 minutes. Value-Added Ratio =
4-20.e: Value-Added Ratio = Process Time / Lead Time = 30 / 300 = 10%. Only 10% of total lead time is spent adding value. The remaining 90% is non-value-added (waiting, transport, inspection, etc.) — a major lean improvement opportunity.

4-20.q: The Future State Map in Value Stream Mapping represents:
o: The Ideal process after waste elimination and improvements
4-20.e: The Future State Map shows the Ideal Process after implementing lean improvements — eliminating identified waste, reducing lead time, improving flow. It is the Target to work toward after documenting the current state.

4-20.q: Cycle time is defined as:
o: Total time from order to delivery
Time to complete One Unit through a process
4-20.e: Cycle time = time to complete One Unit from start to finish in a process step. It is shorter than lead time (which includes all waiting, inspection, and transport in addition to actual processing time).

4-20.q: Lead Time Includes which time that Cycle Time does Not?
4-20.e: Lead Time = Cycle Time + all non-value-added time (Waiting between steps, Transport between locations, Inspection time, etc.). Lead time is always greater than or equal to cycle time. Reducing waiting reduces lead time.

4-20.q: A Value-Added Ratio of 5% means:
o: Only 5% of total lead time is value-added — 95% is waste
4-20.e: Value-Added Ratio = Process Time / Lead Time = 5% means only 5% of total lead time is actually Adding Value. 95% is non-value-added (waiting, transport, inspection). Lean goal: increase this ratio by eliminating waste.

4-20.q: The Current State Map in Value Stream Mapping shows:
o: The process As IT IS Today — including all waste
4-20.e: Current State Map documents the process As IT Exists Today — showing all activities, inventory levels, waiting times, and waste. It is the Starting Point for identifying improvement opportunities.

4-20.q: To increase overall throughput, a Toc manager should focus on improving:
o: The last step in the process
4-20.e: Only improving the Bottleneck increases overall throughput. Improving non-bottleneck steps wastes resources — the bottleneck still limits output. Focus All improvement efforts on the constraint until it is no longer the bottleneck.

4-21.q: Employee quality training is an example of which quality cost category?
4-21.e: Quality training is a Prevention cost — it is incurred Before defects occur to prevent them from happening. Prevention costs are proactive investments in quality.

4-21.q: Product inspection during production is an example of:
4-21.e: Inspection during or after production is an Appraisal cost — its purpose is to Detect defects before they reach the customer. Appraisal costs include testing, inspection, and auditing.

4-21.q: Scrap and rework costs are classified as:
4-21.e: Scrap and rework are Internal Failure costs — defects discovered Inside the company before reaching the customer. These are costly but less expensive than external failures.

4-21.q: Customer warranty claims and product recalls are:
4-21.e: Warranty claims and recalls are External Failure costs — defects that reached the customer. These are the Most Expensive quality costs because they damage customer relationships and company reputation.

4-21.q: Costs of Conformance include:
4-21.e: Costs of Conformance = Prevention + Appraisal. These are costs of Doing IT Right — investing to prevent defects and detect them early. Non-conformance costs = Internal + External Failure (cost of doing it wrong).

4-21.q: According to Tqm philosophy, the responsibility for quality belongs to:
4-21.e: Tqm (Total Quality Management) philosophy holds that quality is Everyone'S responsibility — from top management to front-line workers. Quality is built in from the start, not inspected in at the end.

4-21.q: Kaizen is best described as:
o: A statistical quality tool targeting 3.4 defects per million
A philosophy of continuous small improvements
A method for allocating quality costs
4-21.e: Kaizen is the Japanese philosophy of Continuous Small Improvements. 'Kai' = change, 'Zen' = good/better. It emphasizes constant incremental improvements by all employees rather than occasional large changes.

4-21.q: How does Kaizen costing differ from standard costing?
o: Standard costing sets cost Reduction targets each period; Kaizen costing maintains fixed standards
Kaizen costing sets cost Reduction targets each period; standard costing focuses on maintaining the current standard
Kaizen costing is only used in service industries
They are identical — both use the same variance analysis
4-21.e: Kaizen costing sets ongoing cost Reduction targets — the goal is to continuously lower costs below current actuals. Standard costing sets standards to Maintain — variances flag deviations from standard. Kaizen is about improvement; standard costing is about control.

4-21.q: Kaizen costing is applied primarily:
o: During the product design phase before production begins
During the production phase — improving existing processes incrementally
Only during the annual budgeting process
At the end of the product life cycle
4-21.e: Kaizen costing is applied During The Production Phase — after the product is designed and in production, teams make continuous small improvements to reduce costs incrementally. This contrasts with Target Costing which is applied at the Design stage.

4-21.q: Current actual unit cost $50. Kaizen reduction target 4% per quarter. Kaizen target cost =
4-21.e: Kaizen Target = Current Cost × (1 - Reduction Rate) = $50 × (1 - 0.04) = $50 × 0.96 = $48 per unit. If actual cost for the quarter is $48 or less, the kaizen target was achieved. This new $48 becomes next quarter's baseline.

4-21.q: If a company increases spending on Prevention costs, the expected long-term effect is:
o: Internal and external failure costs Decrease
4-21.e: More prevention spending → fewer defects → Less rework, scrap, warranty claims, and recalls. Prevention is the most cost-effective quality investment. Total quality costs should Decrease over time with a strong prevention program.

4-21.q: The Most Expensive category of quality costs is:
4-21.e: External Failure is the Most Expensive category because defects have already reached customers. Costs include warranty repairs, returns, recalls, legal liability, and — most expensively — Lost Customers and damaged reputation.

4-21.q: Six Sigma quality standard targets:
4-21.e: Six Sigma targets 3.4 defects per Million opportunities — statistically 6 standard deviations from the mean. It uses Dmaic (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) methodology for process improvement.

4-21.q: Total Productive Maintenance (Tpm) aims for:
o: Maximum production speed regardless of quality
Zero Breakdowns, Zero Defects, and Zero Accidents through proactive, employee-driven maintenance
4-21.e: Tpm aims for Zero Breakdowns, Zero Defects, Zero Accidents. It makes maintenance Everyone's responsibility — operators perform routine maintenance (autonomous maintenance). The goal: move from reactive (fix when broken) to proactive (prevent failures) maintenance.

4-21.q: Overall Equipment Effectiveness (Oee) is calculated as:
4-21.e: Oee = Availability × Performance Rate × Quality Rate. 100% Oee means the equipment is running all the time (availability), at full speed (performance), producing only good units (quality). Oee is the key metric in Tpm programs.

4-21.q: A Pareto diagram applies the principle that:
o: 80% of problems come from 20% of causes
4-21.e: Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule): ~80% of quality problems are caused by ~20% of issues. Pareto diagram ranks defects by frequency — management should focus on the Vital Few causes that create most problems.

4-21.q: An Ishikawa (fishbone) diagram is used to:
o: Rank defects from most to least common
Identify root causes of a quality problem
Monitor whether a process is in control
4-21.e: Ishikawa (Cause-and-Effect / Fishbone) diagram maps Potential Root Causes. The 4 M categories: Manpower, Methods, Machines, Materials. Helps teams systematically identify where quality problems originate.

4-21.q: Iso 9001 certification confirms that a company has:
o: A documented and consistently managed quality management system
4-21.e: Iso 9001 certifies a Documented, Consistently Managed quality management System — not defect-free products. It is a Process certification confirming quality processes are properly defined and maintained.

4-21.q: Points outside control limits on a Statistical Process Control chart indicate:
o: An out-of-control process with assignable causes
4-21.e: Points Outside control limits = Out-Of-Control process with Assignable (special) causes that can be identified and corrected. Points Within limits = normal random variation (common causes) = process is in control.

4-21.q: Quality Circles are best described as:
o: Small employee groups identifying and solving quality problems
4-21.e: Quality Circles = small groups of front-line Employees who meet regularly to identify, analyze, and solve quality problems in their area. Key Tqm element — empowers workers to improve their own processes.

4-21.q: Dmaic stands for:
4-21.e: Dmaic is the Six Sigma improvement methodology: Define (the problem), Measure (current performance), Analyze (root causes), Improve (implement solutions), Control (sustain improvements). Used for improving Existing processes.

4-21.q: Increasing Prevention costs typically leads to:
o: Lower internal and external failure costs over time
4-21.e: Prevention investment → fewer defects → Less scrap, rework (internal failure) and warranty claims, returns, recalls (external failure). Prevention is the most cost-effective quality investment. Total quality costs Decrease when prevention spending increases.
